data.title: High Yield Vs. Dividend Growth: Why 2026 Is The Year You Can Have Both
data.description:
Co-authored with Beyond Saving

There is a frequent discussion amongst investors about which is "better" – high yield or dividend growth. Young investors are often lured by the idea of buying a company, holding it for 20 years, and the dividend growing every single year.

It is a very appealing picture that has a few practical problems. For starters, predicting is hard, especially about the future. It is difficult enough to predict that a company is still going to be in business in 20 years and that it won't get bought, go private, merge, or even go bankrupt. Many things can change over 20 years.

The second problem is that dividend growth takes a long time. A 3% yield growing at an annual rate of 10% will take 14 years of dividend growth to reach a 10% yield. So you have a choice: you can invest at 3% today, and maybe you'll have a 10% yield on your capital in a decade or two. Or you can invest in holdings that have a 10% yield right now, and you'll get 10% next year.

It is true that investments with high yields do tend to pay out most of their earnings. As a result, high-yield investments will sometimes reduce their dividends as conditions change. If a company is paying out 100% of earnings, and earnings decline, then they will have to cut their distribution. In the case of RICs (Regulated Investment Companies), companies are required by tax law to distribute the majority of their taxable income. As a result, they can't maintain a large cushion between earnings and their payout, and dividends should be cut when earnings decline to avoid dilution.

The flip side of that reality is that investments that tend to pay out a high portion of their earnings will increase their distributions when their earnings climb. Today, we are going to look at two companies that have cut their dividends in the past when their earnings went through a downcycle. In 2026, we expect both of these companies to see higher earnings, and that will lead to dividend raises.

Pick #1: BRSP—Yield 10.7%

BrightSpire Capital, Inc. (BRSP) is a mortgage REIT that owns mortgages secured by commercial real estate. Over the past two years, many commercial real estate owners have struggled to pay their mortgages. As a result, BRSP has spent the past few years foreclosing on properties.

Since June 2023, BRSP has foreclosed on eight properties with a combined total of $241 million in net carrying value: Source

One of the benefits of the mortgage REIT structure is that when they take over a property, they can actively manage it. Unlike banks, which generally look to dump the asset at any price, an mREIT can operate the property for years if necessary to achieve a beneficial exit price.

When the property is foreclosed on, it is written down to "fair value," which is usually going to be quite low for a property that failed to pay its mortgage. The mREIT then has a variety of options to improve the value, ranging from leasing it up, renovating it, or simply waiting for the market cycle to turn and investor interest in the property to increase. They can sell it quickly to extract capital or wait for the next market peak to sell at a premium price. It requires a constant analysis of opportunity costs. Is the REIT better off selling the property and freeing up capital to make new loans? Or is it better off being patient and possibly selling the property for more in the future?

The bottom line is that, due to pulling in its horns to foreclose on properties and manage other distressed loans, BRSP wasn't originating many loans. Its portfolio as of Q3 2025 was $2.4 billion, when it was over $3.9 billion in Q3 2022. A smaller portfolio produces fewer earnings.

On the Q3 2025 earnings call, CEO Mazzei noted that they are targeting a $3.5 billion portfolio in 2026:

"Our timetable for sale of REO assets will generate liquidity for future loan originations and drive the loan book growth toward our targeted portfolio of approximately $3.5 billion."

To achieve that target, management expects to need to originate about $300 million/quarter because other loans are expected to be repaid. We know BRSP hit that goal in Q4 because they reached it before they announced Q3 earnings:

When BRSP reports its Q4 earnings, we will be looking to see if they are on track to start out the year and if management still thinks it can hit that $3.5 billion mark.

If management can achieve that goal in 2026, there is a high probability that we will see a dividend raise late in 2026 or early 2027 as BRSP ramps up its leverage. Sometimes it really is as simple as a larger portfolio that generates more cash.

Pick #2: EPR—Yield 6.3%

Last year, I didn't take a vacation. At least not a vacation in the sense of traveling to do fun things. Life happened, and time slipped away. This year, some vacations are being planned. But don't worry, my work travels with me!

In this case, I think it is vital to do some due diligence on some properties owned by EPR Properties (EPR). One thing I have never tried is skydiving, and EPR owns several iFLY Indoor Skydiving locations. About 25 years ago, give or take, I fell in love with Pagosa Springs. It is definitely a place worth going back to a time or ten, and The Springs Resort & Spa is owned by EPR.

EPR Properties is a REIT that owns places that are simply fun to go to. Whether you want to hit balls at a TopGolf (which fortunately requires zero golfing skills), do some bouldering at a climbing gym in Chicago, explore the arches of Moab, enjoy a day at the waterpark, or simply enjoy dinner and a movie, EPR is there.

EPR Properties was originally founded as a movie theater REIT. It owned properties that were leased to AMC—which, despite the turmoil in the industry, from a landlord perspective, theaters have been good to EPR. Occupancy has routinely been +99%, and outside the disruption of COVID, rent has been paid on time. The back rent that was deferred during COVID was all paid back. EPR's focus on high-quality locations means that even when Cineworld (branded as Regal in the U.S.) filed bankruptcy, they had fewer properties closed than other REITs that had a fraction of its exposure to Regal.

However, it is a reality that movie theaters aren't as popular as they once were. The model is moving to fewer theaters that offer premium offerings, as opposed to theaters that relied on high volumes. For several years, EPR has slowly reduced its exposure to theaters.

Theaters are still the largest exposure at 37% of annualized adjusted EBITDA, but their largest tenant, Callaway Golf (CALY), is in the "Eat & Play" segment. EPR was the first REIT to start investing in the development of TopGolf locations when it was a start-up company, and the relationship has continued to grow. Source

EPR isn't rushing to dump theaters; it is diversifying primarily through investing elsewhere. We expect that their diversification will accelerate in 2026, as management has laid out a plan to invest $400-$500 million in 2026. Since COVID, EPR has not meaningfully grown its portfolio. Initially, it was simply recovering from the impacts of COVID, and then interest rates were sky-high, and management didn't believe their cost of capital was appropriate for aggressive expansion.

As we go into 2026, EPR has the opportunity to invest at low prices (higher cap rates) thanks to high interest rates, and it has a strong balance sheet to leverage. Source

EPR has room to grow its dividend, and we expect a small dividend raise in 2026. Better yet, we believe that AFFO will be able to grow at a pace closer to 5%, which will drive more significant dividend growth in the future as well as potential for price upside.

EPR is currently trading at a low valuation, about 10x forward AFFO. This is a REIT that is capable of trading at a valuation of 16x AFFO, which would be around $85.

We see a lot to love about buying EPR at current valuations. It's ready to grow after a few years of not doing much. That means higher valuations, higher share prices, and a growing dividend.

Plus, the benefit of that really cool feeling while you're sitting at an EPR property and thinking to yourself, "This place is paying me dividends!" Maybe that isn't the best reason to buy a stock, but it is one of the best feelings in the world.

Conclusion

Do you want to invest in dividend growth or high yield? Why not both? BRSP shrank its portfolio as commercial real estate defaults were piling up. A smaller portfolio led to lower earnings. Late last year, BRSP started ramping up its originations, and we expect BRSP to grow its portfolio by nearly $1 billion in 2026. That will drive higher earnings and lead to the dividend returning to growth.

EPR is a REIT that was hit really hard by COVID and suspended its dividend to protect its balance sheet. Perhaps it isn't a popular move, but it was definitely the smart and responsible move. Following the pandemic, management took a cautious approach to expansion as high interest rates led to an unattractive cost of capital. With interest rates coming down, EPR has signaled that they expect to expand aggressively in 2026. Thanks to their prudent balance sheet management, EPR has ample room to expand responsibly.

As investors, we can get a very healthy yield starting out, and we can expect to see dividend growth in the near future. The best of both worlds!

data.title: Synopsys: Catalysts Are Still Far Away, Wait For A Better Price
data.description:
Investment thesis

Synopsys, Inc. (SNPS) is a software company that its product is used by chip manufacturers to design chips. This industry has been dominated by three players: Synopsys, Cadence Design Systems (CDNS), and Siemens EDA. Synopsys and Cadence stock prices have usually traded at par. As you see in Figure 1, both stocks trade with a gap. That could be an opportunity to buy Synopsys until it gets to the level of Cadence.

I love this business. I am an investor in Synopsys and Cadence. I think there are some catalysts that could raise Synopsys stock price. Its IP business has deteriorated, and I think the company has the levers to improve it and regain its leadership. In the EDA software, Ansys integration can give synergies and capture more value from both solutions integrated. And new AI agentic capabilities can raise even more the value of the solutions as they can replace even more engineers.

However, the value of Synopsys in my estimation is $478.5 per share, down $7.3 per share from its current stock price. Risks are moderate and dependent on external factors and could affect the complete industry. I recommend holding and waiting for a further downturn in the stock because the catalysts won’t appear this year.

The IP business has been a growth driver for the company, and the problems that it has faced, which have hammered the stock price, are an opportunity.

Synopsys’ IP business decreased 8% in 2025 vs. 2024, to around $155 million, with total revenue at $1.7 billion. Operating margin decreased even more, 43% reaching $419 million of adjusted operating income due to the fixed costs of employees developing the solutions. Those results are highly impacted by the US restrictions on China, among others.

Management has acknowledged the problems, especially those that relate to its mismanagement, such as wrong resource allocation. That sincerity gives me confidence in a turnaround. In the long term, I am confident that China’s restrictions will fade away as the problems are provoked by a one-of-a-kind president in a democratic regime. Another leader will have a more diplomatic way of negotiation. Synopsys is a leader in the IP business, being almost twice that of Cadence. Cadence didn’t alert over those problems, and what has stated is that “strong momentum, driven by global accelerating IP demand”. I consider that the IP business, greater in Synopsys, made a much greater impact than EDA software. And Synopsys IP business, more related to the foundry business, was more impacted by regulatory restrictions. Whatever, my impression is that other factors, like mismanagement, have beaten down its revenue and margins in the IP business rather than restrictions in China. However, I consider that Synopsys will be able to catch up with Cadence and reestablish its IP business.

I am also confident that the new royalty model in custom IP will boost Ansys’ multiples. The company is trying to change from NRE (Non-Recurring Engineering) + Use Fee to add a royalty that will increase revenue, as the company would get a royalty for each piece sold. ARM Holdings (ARM), which has a similar model, trades at 67.41 vs 34.86 in the case of Synopsys, Figure 3. Anyway, success in this transformation is not assured, and the impact will take time as you recognize revenue of the fee right away, but royalties are cashed 2-3 years later when the product is launched to the market.

EDA: two driving forces

I believe the EDA software business has a lot of fuel to increase its pricing power and contribute to solid revenue growth in the coming years. The integration with Ansys and the agentic capabilities that will change the way the software is used and the value created.

I believe that the original Synopsys EDA software and Ansys software create more value when integrated than as two separate pieces of software. Nowadays, a designer loses time because they first design the semiconductor logic, Synopsys, and then use another software, Ansys, to check the heat, for example. However, if the heat doesn’t fit, then it has to change the whole logic design. This loop could take weeks. With the integration, the designer just has to make a complete design of the semiconductor. The EDA value is based on time-to-market instead of the software. Besides, the integration will eliminate any blind spots due to friction in the transfer of the design from one software to another.

The other driving force is AI's agentic new capability. Basically, the agentic software would do the work the engineers are currently doing. If a work employs 10 engineers with a cost of $5 million, Synopsis could claim could claim $2 million if the software saves half of the engineers. Just to grasp how much revenue could be lifted, if the price could increase 20%-30% for 30% of the customers in three years, it could increase revenue growth by 2-3 percentage points. I am based on a 15-20% increase in bookings and revenue in DSO.ai. The value for Synopsys’ customers is high, especially in the current context of a shortage of engineers. Making the engineers’ work easier, the cost of engineers will decrease, as well as productivity. As AI agentic software would be more intensive, it is an engineer who could work 24 hours, the hardware’s revenue will increase too, otherwise the cloud cost would be high.

Valuation

In my valuation methodology, I discount projected free cash flows at a convenient WACC. I define cash margin as net income adjusted for non-cash items such as amortization and depreciation, stock-based compensation, and deferred income tax, offering a clearer view of operational cash generation than standard margin metrics (Figure 4).

For 2026, I foresee $9.594 billion influenced by the acquisition of Anys and in line with analysts’ estimates. Later on, I estimate a 10% growth rate in 2027, which will increase to 15% in my ten-year model, higher than the long-term management guidance of 12-13%. This steady increase is supported by a transformation in the IP business and a new model based on royalties. In EDA software, it is supported by getting more value with an integrated solution with Ansys and the development of a new model with AI agentic, as I have stated.

Cash Margin will grow very slightly from 34% to 36% in the ten-year period. For those growth drivers, we have talked about the company must invest in research and development even more than the historical 35%. However, I see improvements due to the integration of Ansys. Net working capital and CAPEX investment will be in line with the historical value. Especially, net working capital changes are difficult to predict.

I estimate Synopsys value at $478.5 per share, down 7.3% compared to the current stock price (Figure 5). The Price-to-Free Cash Flow is 67.8, which is even higher compared to historical values (Figure 6). The company follows the rule of 40 as a good software company, but the free cash flow yield in 5 years is 3%, down from the risk-free rate of 4%.

Figure 6

Risks

The main risk for Synopsys is new restrictions in China. In the current volatile political context, I consider a 65% probability of occurrence. As we have shown, the IP business has decreased by 8% in revenue and 43% in adjusted operating income. Maybe a new restriction won’t damage as much as the previous one, as the company will be more protected, and the easiest business has already left. I could see clearly $200 million a year in free cash flow impact. Here, what is important is that in the former restriction, Chinese start-ups have emerged, and it is currently a threat because those start-ups will keep developing solutions up-market.

There is an exogenous risk that depends on the hyperscalers’ investment. If there is a deceleration in investment, there will be less chip investment in companies like Nvidia (NVDA), AMD (AMD),…, which means fewer designs and less revenue for Synopsis and Cadence across the industry. Hyperscaler CAPEX is expected to reach $602 billion, a +36% increase year over year. I don’t expect this movement to be disruptive, but a more progressive way. And I don’t expect this year, and maybe next year, to occur. So, the probability of occurrence is lower, maybe 20%.

Conclusion

Even though my future prospects in the IP business and the EDA business are quite positive, the impact of those catalysts is not immediate, and it will take years. So, I don’t expect the stock price to get a boost and even catch up with Cadence. The IP business will take a year to regain past success, and royalties will take longer, about two or three more years. Integration with Ansys and agentic software capabilities is also one or two years apart. I would wait, and if the price reaches $478.5 per share, as my target price, I would buy; otherwise, I recommend holding.

data.title: ENFR: Midstream Energy Fund, Good 4.5% Dividend Yield, Not So Good Entry Point
data.description:
ENFR - Overview and Analysis

Index and Portfolio

The Alerian Energy Infrastructure ETF (ENFR) is a midstream energy index ETF, tracking the Alerian Midstream Energy Select Index. It is a relatively simple index, including midstream energy corporations and MLPs located in either the U.S. or Canada.

Midstream companies are those focusing on gathering, processing, liquefaction, transportation, and storage of energy products, including crude oil, natural gas, LNG, and others. It is a modified market-cap weighted index, with caps meant to ensure compliance with regulatory standards. ENFR is not liable for corporate taxes, nor do investors have to file a K-1 form.

ENFR provides diversified exposure to midstream energy companies, which isn't saying much - this is a niche subsector of a small equity sector. It invests in 25 different securities, much lower than most equity ETFs. These are as follows:

Although ENFR provides diversified exposure to its investment niche, it does not provide broad-based exposure to the energy sector as a whole, much less to broader U.S. equity markets. ENFR's performance could materially differ from that of its index, and much more so than average. Correlations between the S&P 500 and ENFR are lower than those of most sectors I've seen, including tech (the largest):

Due to the above, I would keep any investment in ENFR small and would make sure to not significantly overweight energy in my portfolio. Smaller positions and some tilts seem better, in my opinion at least.

Revenues, earnings, and cash-flows for blue-chip midstream energy companies are only slightly dependent on energy prices. Blue-chip midstream companies focus on fee-for-service and similar arrangements, with long-term contracts and zero direct commodity price exposure.

As an example, compare EBITDA and cash from operations for ENFR's three largest holdings, with those of Exxon and Chevron during 2020, when energy prices collapsed.

As can be seen above, the three midstream energy companies above saw single-digit declines in their EBITDA and cash from operations during the pandemic. Bad results, but much better than diversified oil majores, which saw declines of around 50% in everything. That is a massive difference, and an incredibly important issue for investors to consider.

On a more negative note, investors don't seem to see things the same way, with most midstream energy companies trading alongside the oil majors when times are tough. Compare their returns during 2020, for instance.

As can be seen above, although there was quite a bit of variation in their performance, midstream saw significant losses during the pandemic, not too disimilar from the oil majors, and far in excess of their financial losses. Although I do not believe said price movement to be all that rational, that is how the market moved, and how the market tends to move during these events. The fact that blue-chip midstream energy companies are less exposed to energy companies matters, as does the fact that the matter does not seem to care all that much about this.

Importantly, not all midstream energy companies behave as above. Some companies, generally the smaller ones, focus on activities or contracts with more significant energy price exposure, seeing significant deterioration in their financials when oil prices decline. I believe that Energy Transfer (ET) is one of these, even though it's a massive company, and even though the GAAP performance was reasonably good during 2020. I do recall ET suffering from significant issues at the time, and the dividends were slashed too:

ENFR invests in over a dozen midstream energy companies, some with significant energy exposure, some with little direct exposure. Overall, I would say the fund has a moderate amount of energy price exposure, although I don't have any figures here.

Dividend Yield

Midstream energy companies tend to distribute excess cash-flows to shareholders as dividends or distributions and generally have good dividend yields. ENFR itself yields 4.6%, a good dividend yield for an equity ETF, quite a bit higher than the equity average, slightly higher than the energy average.

Obviously most fixed-income securities and some niche income producing assets yield more than ENFR, some a lot more, but ENFR is an equity ETF, and could see significant capital gains, much more so than most investments yielding materially more than 4.5%.

ENFR's dividends have seen strong growth since inception, and during most time periods. Lots of exceptions, and distributions do tend to get cut during downturns, recessions, and commodity price crunches.

Dividend growth figures above are accurate, but I don't believe they capture the entirety of the fund's dividend volatility. As an example, dividends decreased by more than 30% in the 2022, something not really noticeable in the figures above.

On a more negative note, ENFR currently trades with a somewhat below-average dividend yield. Dividend yields were much higher during 2020 - 2024, although consistently lower than before the pandemic. Valuations collapsed during the pandemic, energy prices increased during the recovery, explaining these results.

Overall, I'm apprehensive about investing in the fund considering the above. It simply does not look like the best time to buy, buying now means locking-in a comparatively weak yield, weaker than during most of the past five years. Seems risky, and expected income and returns simply do not look all that great.

Valuation

Midstream energy companies have seen their prices and valuations increase these past few years. Ratios are all much higher than they were in early 2024, as per data from this older article of mine:

Valuations for energy companies more broadly are currently at historically above-average levels. Same is probably true of midstream energy companies, but obviously I can't say for certain. Same is definitely true for the broader equity market though.

Overall, this does not look like a good time to buy ENFR. Prices are a bit bad, even though not terrible, dividend yields do not look attractive either. Energy is an incredibly cyclical business, so risks seem even higher accounting for that. At the same time, I don't really see a catalyst for a massive improvement in industry conditions, or in valuations. Economic conditions are such that I could see a (shallow) recession occurring, perhaps a minor increase in inflation from continued tariff pressure. Significantly higher oil prices seem unlikely, I'm not really seeing any potential increase in demand, nor reduction in supply or, more likely, supply chain issues.

On a more positive note, looking at the graph above I'm seeing very good buyback and dividend yields for energy companies. Both are of particular importance to energy companies, as these leverage / are more impactful when valuations are low, which is truer of energy companies than most equities, and because returning significant amounts of cash to shareholders all but precludes the possibility of a massive CAPEX run, making it difficult for oil prices to collapse (smaller price movements are a lot more possible).

Performance Track-Record

ENFR's performance is, well, somewhat complicated.

Long-term performance is mediocre, with the fund significantly underperforming the S&P 500 since inception.

Underperformance was almost entirely concentrated before the pandemic, with the fund effectively matching, marginally underperforming, the S&P 500 since. Returns below are from early 2020 onwards, so just before oil prices collapse. Timing is horrible, returns about average.

Volatility is solidly above-average, as are drawdowns (see above). Due bear in mind that actual underlying earnings volatility is much lower, but share prices definitely aren't.

Recent results are stronger, with the fund significantly outperforming since my first article in late 2021, moderately so since my most recent article:

Overall, ENFR's performance seems reasonable enough to me, although I'm sure some will find the fund's mediocre long-term returns to be a deal-breaker.

Conclusion

This was a tough call for me. ENFR's valuation does look a bit stretched, but the same is true of the broader equities market. I'm not seeing any positive catalysts, but I'm not really bearish either. The price was better earlier in the month, but not significantly so. Settled on a buy due to general bullishness, and because the negative issues here don't seem to be significant enough as to be a deal-breaker.

data.title: Deep Sail Capital Q4 2025 Investor Letter
data.description:
Investors,

In the fourth quarter of 2025, Deep Sail Capital Partners (the "Fund") returned 14.2% net of fees while averaging 84% net long exposure. For the full year 2025 the fund has returned 34.8% net of fees while averaging 82% net long exposure. Please consult your individual capital account statements for your individual net returns.

In the fourth quarter, the fund outperformed both of its benchmarks, the Russell 2000 Mid Cap Growth Index and the Russell 2000 Index. This capped a second consecutive year of significant relative performance. For the full year 2025, the fund exceeded both benchmarks by more than 10%, following a similarly strong year in 2024. Deep Sail Capital returned 38% in 2024 and 34.8% in 2025, results I am proud of but do not take for granted. Equally important to the funds performance, these two years reinforced invaluable lessons around disciplined risk management, portfolio construction, timing, and navigating markets amid persistent political and macroeconomic uncertainty. I believe this combination of strong performance and continuous learning has meaningfully strengthened our investment process going forward.

In the fourth quarter both the long portfolio and the short portfolio positively contributed to the performance. The long portfolio was driven in large part by a few positions with significant outperformance. The short portfolio has come under significant pressure multiple times in 2025, but finally in the fourth quarter the lowest-quality, longest-duration equities we have been short finally took a significant leg down, as the bubble in these retail favorite names deflated.

Market Commentary – Run it hot

In our last few quarterly commentaries, I have continued to point out the negatives of the Trump-led economy and warn of future economic woes. This commentary is no different.

It’s time to accept we are about to “RUN IT HOT.” Once Trump appoints a new Fed Chair, the odds tilt toward an explicitly run it hot economic stance that prioritizes growth, loose fiscal policy, and lower interest rates over inflation control. A Trump-appointed Fed chairman would no doubt be slower to hike rates and quicker to ease. Trump clearly has no real understanding of how the economy works; all he knows is what the profiteers around him tell him.

In the short term expect some rocket fuel on the economy: credit flows more freely, asset prices inflate, consumer confidence holds up, and headline growth looks strong. Markets tend to love this phase, and so does any administration eager to showcase momentum. Too bad Trump is too dumb to time this right, and the economy will no doubt falter from this policy before the next presidential election, likely costing the Republicans the election.

The trade-off for this rocket fuel is higher inflation and a lower dollar. Running the economy above its sustainable speed almost guarantees upward pressure on prices, particularly if supply-side constraints, tariffs, or deglobalization remain core Trump policies. A dovish or growth-tolerant Fed erodes real yields, making USD-denominated assets less attractive at the margin and pushing the dollar lower. A weaker USD can further stoke inflation via higher import prices, creating a feedback loop that’s easy to dismiss early on but hard to rein in later. For a while, this still will feel fine, likely good for most Americans, but it can only last a few quarters.

Eventually, though, the realities of economics show up. Inflation doesn’t just raise prices; it forces economic realities, reduces real incomes for working Americans, and distorts the capital allocation process. At the same time as this is all happening while there is structural job loss going on driven in part by AI-driven automation. Companies are refusing to hire new roles as they look to automate more workloads with AI.

This trend will only get worse over time, as it is a true structural change in the labor force that will occur over several years. It will continue to push unemployment higher even as prices remain elevated. That’s the uncomfortable endgame: inflation that’s too high to ignore combined with weakening labor markets that make tightening politically and economically painful. The short-term rocket fuel will also increase home prices as interest rates fall, making homes even less affordable. This all gives way to a harder landing, where the tools to fix the problem are blunter, the costs are higher, and the margin for error is much thinner.

Until then, I’m maintaining discipline, protecting capital, and waiting for the market to reveal when “risk on” finally becomes “risk off.”

Long Portfolio Summary

In the fourth quarter, the long portfolio outperformed both of our benchmarks, the Russell 2000 Index and the Russell Midcap Growth Index, by a significant margin. The largest contributor in Q4 was Beyond Meat, which was a shortly held special situation short squeeze that returned 350% in the matter of a few days. The opportunity set for short squeezes is very limited, but as an avid watcher of highly shorted stocks, they arise occasionally, and the fund’s strategy is to take advantage of those opportunities on the long side. I wouldn’t count on this type of one off returns from shorts squeezes to become a regular occurrence within the fund but rather a positive situation every few years.

Again in Q4, our largest position, Kraken Robotics, continued to have a high contribution to the fund, contributing a 7% gross return. The company continues gaining widespread awareness from investors and new orders from its UAV partners, which is propelling the stock upward. I continue to believe the company is perfectly positioned within their market, especially as Andruil makes its way to an IPO in 2026. In Q4, the fund closed three positions and opened three new positions. The new positions in Q4 included Credo Technology Group and NameSilo Technologies.

Current Position: Sanuwave Health – Emerging Growth

Sanuwave health (SNWV) is a specialty medical device company with an incredible opportunity in front of it. Sanuwave is the maker and distributor of UltraMist, a low- frequency, non-contact ultrasound energy device that delivers wound care treatment through a fluid mist. UltraMist is FDA approved and reimbursed through Nationwide CMS reimbursement via a therapy specific CPT code.

The investment thesis for Sanuwave Health, Inc. centers on a fundamental shift in the advanced wound care ecosystem, transitioning from high-cost, under-regulated biological grafts toward energy-based tissue regeneration. As the medical community and federal payers grapple with a multi-billion dollar surge in unsustainable spending on skin substitutes, Sanuwave has positioned its directed energy platforms with UltraMIST as a high-value alternative.

Sanuwave utilizes a "razor blade" recurring revenue model that has achieved record quarterly revenues throughout 2025, culminating in a preliminary full-year revenue projection of $44.3 million to $44.4 million (36% y-o-y increase). This growth is underpinned by the UltraMIST system, which provides non-contact, low-frequency ultrasound to stimulate cellular-level healing. The DermaPACE system, the first shockwave therapy to receive FDA clearance for diabetic foot ulcers (DFUs), is also a part of the product portfolio but is not a major driver in the company's growth. The management team and company were recently restructured under CEO Morgan Frank. Morgan is a hedge fund guy, who has run the Manchester Explorer Fund for the last 23 years. Morgan spent the last few years building a sales and operating team with veterans from high-growth medical technology firms like Abiomed and Healogics. In late 2024 the company successfully transformed the company’s capital structure through a warrant conversion and reverse stock split. Then in 2025 the company refinanced its debt with JPMorgan Chase eliminated legacy high-interest debt, reducing the cost of capital and providing the liquidity necessary for commercial scaling. All of these actions have set the company up for a multi year path of growth with a high free cashflow conversion.

The competitive landscape is currently undergoing a significant disruption due to the Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services (CMS) final rule for 2026. This regulation reduces Medicare spending on skin substitutes by approximately 90%, or $19.6 billion, by reclassifying them as "incident-to" supplies and imposing a flat reimbursement rate. This change caused much uncertainty in 2025 in the market, as users of all wound care products shifted their buying patterns to adjust for the change. But over the long term I view the changes as positive for Sanuwave, as biological competitors struggle with the reimbursement changes Sanuwave’s UltraMIST product will maintain a stable and preferred reimbursement profile under CPT code 97610. With an addressable market for advanced wound care projected to reach $17.8 billion by 2033 and a business model producing 78% gross margins, Sanuwave represents a compelling asymmetric opportunity for investors.

High quality business model

The quality of Sanuwave’s business model is due to a combination of high gross margins and a recurring revenue model built on a deeply entrenched installed base of UltraMIST systems, currently approaching 2000 in use. The company has been largely restructured in the last few years and it is set to enter into a phase of high operating leverage high sales growth.

Sanuwave’s primary revenue driver is the UltraMIST system, which consists of a durable generator and a single-use, disposable applicator for each patient treatment (the razor blade). The best part about this razor/razor blade model is the UltraMIST system has gross margins around ~70%, while the razor blades are in the 80-90% range. That means both the razor and the razor blades are sold at extremely high gross margins. Gross margins reached 77.9% in the third quarter of 2025, up from 75.5% in the same period of 2024. This expansion is a direct result of the increasing proportion of high-margin consumables in the revenue mix and the optimization of the company’s supply chain following the move to its new headquarters in Eden Prairie, Minnesota.

2025 was a pivotal year for the company as they turned a positive adjusted EBITDA. By the third quarter of 2025, quarterly adjusted EBITDA had reached $3.5 million, providing the cash flow necessary to service the company’s newly secured institutional debt. Below is a summary of the financial results of the business for the last 3 years.

The business model is protected by a substantial intellectual property portfolio that prevents competitors from easily replicating the "Energy First" protocol. Sanuwave holds 233 total patent documents, including grants and applications, covering the specific delivery mechanisms for both low-frequency ultrasound and electro-hydraulic shockwaves. These patents extend beyond hardware to include the biological response-activating medical systems that stimulate angiogenesis and break down bacterial biofilms.

In 2025, the company demonstrated the intrinsic value of this IP by securing a $5 million payment for the licensing of certain shockwave patents within its intravascular portfolio.

Outstanding management

The second pillar of the investment thesis is the presence of an institutional-grade leadership team. Sanuwave has undergone a deliberate leadership transformation to align its management capabilities with its commercial ambitions. Morgan Frank is the core of this transition. I spoke with Morgan for the first time in early 2025, and I was instantly impressed. His knowledge of investing, capital allocation, and excitement for the UltraMIST product opportunity are very clear when you speak with him. Morgan has a wealth of investing experience. He is currently a principal at Manchester Management. Since taking over as CEO, he has brought a capital markets discipline that was previously absent from the company. He has taken a great product and a bad company situation and converted it to a great product and a great company situation. His tenure has been marked by several "structural" wins: the 1-for-375 reverse stock split that enabled the Nasdaq up-listing, the relocation of the company to the Minnesota med-tech corridor, and the elimination of the distressed debt.

Morgan has also done a great job of upskilling the organization. The addition of Peter Sorensen as CFO in 2024 provided the financial rigor needed to manage a Nasdaq-listed entity and the JPMorgan Chase credit agreement in 2025.

The sales team expansion and upgrade has been another success. Tim Wern (EVP of Sales) and Dustin Libby (EVP of Commercial Operations) both held leadership roles at Abiomed, a company renowned in the med-tech industry for its aggressive and successful sales culture that scaled from a micro-cap to a Johnson & Johnson acquisition. Wern’s experience in launching transformative technologies and Libby’s track record of scaling surgical businesses from $15 million to $500 million suggest that Sanuwave's sales strategy is modeled after industry best practices. The sales team is finally in a spot in mid-2025 where they can go “elephant hunting” for large customer accounts. I expect we see a few sizable sales deals landed in 2026.

Substantial long term growth prospects

Sanuwave’s growth prospects are defined by a massive TAM and a historic regulatory tailwind that is currently dismantling its primary competition. The company is not merely participating in a growing market; it is actively displacing legacy technologies through a combination of clinical superiority and economic stability.

The global advanced wound care market is estimated at $11.7 billion in 2025 and is projected to grow to $17.8 billion by 2033. This growth is fueled by the relentless rise in the incidence of chronic diseases like diabetes and obesity, which lead to non-healing wounds like diabetic foot ulcers and pressure ulcers. In the United States alone, chronic wounds affect approximately 10 million Medicare beneficiaries and drive billions in annual healthcare costs.

Sanuwave’s growth is outpacing the broader market, which has a CAGR of approximately 5%. Sanuwave’s revenue growth of 60% in 2024 and 36% in 2025 demonstrates significant market share gains. The company is particularly well-positioned in the "active wound care" and "devices" segments, which are expected to witness the fastest growth as clinicians move away from passive dressings toward therapies that actively modulate the wound environment.

The regulatory landscape currently favors UltraMIST in the post-op setting. The CY 2026 PFS final rule represents a tectonic shift. CMS will reclassify most skin substitutes as "incident-to" supplies and move to a flat national payment rate of approximately $127.14 per square centimeter. This is expected to reduce Medicare spending on these products by nearly 90%, effectively ending the "gold rush" for biological grafts, and rewiring the reimbursement landscape. Sanuwave's UltraMIST system is uniquely positioned to benefit from this disruption due to reimbursement stability, operational clarity, and economic incentive alignment.

Sanuwave’s long-term growth is not restricted to chronic wound care. The PACE technology has demonstrated efficacy in several secondary verticals, which could expand the company's TAM as it enters new markets, including orthopedics and endovascular.

Currently the company believes that UltraMIST is still very early in its market penetration, with a single-digit level of penetration. The future of UltraMIST is very bright, and all the pieces are in place for the company to deliver on that future.

Reasonable valuation

Sanuwave’s P/S ratio of ~5.6x is roughly in line with the peer average for high-growth medical equipment companies. For example, Stryker (SYK) trades at 5.7x and DexCom (DXCM) at 6x. However, Sanuwave’s revenue growth of 60% in 2024 and 36% in 2025 is significantly higher than the industry average.

The company's EV/EBITDA ratio, which is currently forecasted at 15x for NTM is extremely low for a company with a +30% revenue growth rate, a 75% gross margin, and a IP protected technology. Due to the fact the company is at the early stage of its profitability, we believe the market is discounting the valuation until the company delivers. As the company scales, the high operating leverage (fixed costs remaining stable while 78% margin revenue grows) is expected to drive exponential EBITDA growth. I believe a fair valuation for this business in in the 25-30x NTM EBITDA range. This suggests that Sanuwave is undervalued on a growth- adjusted basis.

Catalysts and Conclusion

  • 2025 Reimbursement Behind it: Sanuwave hit a bump in 2025 with the changes to the FPS 2026 final rule causing shifts in the wound care business. I believe the industry has ingested those changes and the path forward for Sanuwave is much smoother.
  • Sales Org stand up & Elephant Hunting: The sales organization was reset in 2025 with several key additions of highly experienced reps. They are currently working on focusing on larger customer additions as they “elephant hunt”. I expect 2026 to be a step up in the sales pipeline delivery.

Sanuwave is a rare small cap that ticks all of the boxes that I look for including excellent management, high gross margins, large TAM, and reasonable valuation.

Short Portfolio Summary

The short portfolio performed well in the fourth quarter, as high-quality, long-duration stocks, especially Quantum-related stocks, declined significantly. The short portfolio contributed positively to the overall returns of the funds as the re-bubble I discussed in Q3 has deflated.

As noted in the Market Commentary section, I believe we are currently about to RUN IT HOT, and thus I have dialed down the short exposure in the near term. For now, we are maintaining discipline and waiting for better short entry points to emerge.

Top Holdings & Current Exposure

At the end of the fourth quarter the fund held 19 long positions and 23 short positions. The fund ended the year with an exposure of 122% long and 33% short or an 89% net long exposure.

Sincerely,

Sean

data.title: Axos Financial: A Respectful Downgrade After An Excellent Run Higher
data.description:
During the time that I have been bullish about it, Axos Financial (AX) has had a really remarkable run. For instance, since I last reaffirmed the company as a ‘buy’ candidate back in August of last year, the stock has jumped 12.3%. That means it has outperformed the 9.5% increase that the S&P 500 saw over the same window of time. That alone is good, but what's even better is the performance of the business since I initially rated it a ‘buy’ in December of 2023.

From that time through today, the stock has risen an astounding 92.5%. That's almost double the 49.5% increase that the market experienced. This strong performance has been driven by strong asset quality that has encouraged investors to pay multiples that are, typically, a bit lofty in this space. However, high quality only goes so far. At the end of the day, shares of the business don't look as attractive as they did previously. And it is with that in mind that I believe downgrading it to a ‘hold’ is the appropriate choice.

I am fully willing to accept that this downgrade might be premature. But we will find out one way or the other very soon. This is because, after the market closes on January 29th, the management team at Axos Financial will be announcing financial results for the second quarter of the company's 2026 fiscal year. As we approach that date, it's worth noting that analysts believe that revenue and profits will rise nicely. That could very well change my overall assessment, especially if results come in better than anticipated. But for now, I am confident with downgrading it out of an abundance of caution.

Time to take a step back

Probably the best place to start when it comes to analyzing banks is the balance sheet. And honestly, my favorite part of the balance sheet to look at here would be deposits. As the fuel for the institutions, deposits are incredibly important. And during the most recent quarter, which was the first quarter of the 2026 fiscal year, Axos Financial posted deposits of $22.26 billion. This compares to the $20.83 billion in deposits that the company had at the end of 2025. That's a pretty significant move higher, but it is important to understand why it occurred. A big part of the increase in deposits during this window of time stemmed from an increase in brokered deposits from $1.80 billion to $2.16 billion. Not all deposits are treated equally. Brokered deposits are the costliest since they come from other financial institutions. If we were to strip these out from the equation, deposits would have grown only about $1.08 billion, or 5.7%.

One risk that exists with banks is exposure to uninsured deposits. These are deposits that the FDIC typically does not guarantee. These deposits are subject to bank runs if regulators don't step in during times of crisis. And high amounts of uninsured deposit exposure can destroy a bank during those times. The good news is that while management does not provide an exact figure regarding uninsured deposit exposure, they do say that when you factor in collateralized accounts, overall exposure is around 10%. This is considerably lower than the 30% maximum that I typically like to see.

As deposits increase, loans generally rise as well. During the quarter, Axos Financial had loans of $22.64 billion. That was up from the $21.05 billion the company ended the previous year with. The largest chunk of its loan portfolio would be the $7.98 billion of non-real estate commercial and industrial loans. But this was followed very closely by the $7.30 billion of commercial real estate. Other major categories here include single-family mortgage and warehousing and multifamily and commercial mortgage debt. The firm also has a bit of auto and consumer loans. But on the whole, these account for just 2.3% of the total. I actually view this as a positive since delinquency rates for vehicles are on the rise.

By comparison, most other aspects of the balance sheet are rather small. The company had only $58.3 million in securities during the third quarter of 2026. That was down from $66.7 million at the end of 2025. Cash and cash equivalents did manage to jump from $2.18 billion to $2.89 billion. And at the same time this occurred, debt ballooned from $512.1 million to $1.94 billion. What this tells me is that management is willing to take on additional debt, even if it might impair returns. And as you will see, we did see some signs of that.

Based on the data provided by management, net interest income for the first quarter of 2026 was $273.8 million. That was unfortunately down from the $278 million the company reported previously. This drop was in spite of the fact that the size of the company's balance sheet increased. Some of it can be chalked up to a rise in the provision for credit losses from $14 million to $17.3 million. However, the aforementioned changes in the composition of the balance sheet pushed the net interest margin down from 5.17% to 4.75%. That is still massive compared to most banks, with most of them having a net interest margin of between 2% and 3%. But the overall trajectory there was not necessarily ideal.

Non-interest income has been growing. It increased from $28.6 million in the first quarter of 2025 to $32.3 million at the same time in 2026. This increase was primarily driven by banking and service fee revenue increasing, mortgage banking and servicing rights income growing, and an uptick in advisory fee income. Even though this is still a relatively small portion of the business, the fact that it increased year over year, with that rise driven by multiple items, is a positive and is indicative of the company getting healthier in that regard. Unfortunately, that was not enough to push growth materially higher, with the end result being net profits that barely budged, inching up ever so slightly from $112.3 million to $112.4 million.

Based on the data that we have been given already, we are looking at a company that's trading at a price-to-earnings multiple of about 12. In the chart above, you can see that this makes the institution cheaper than four of the five banks that I compared it to. Unfortunately, that is still above the 10 maximum I look for in the space. The picture when it comes to the price-to-book multiple is considerably worse, as is the picture when it comes to the price-to-tangible book multiple. In the chart below, you can see that four of the five competitors are cheaper than it is in both instances. That is discouraging, as is the fact that the multiples are both around 2. I typically prefer multiples here of around 1 or lower.

Normally, such valuation issues would necessitate a bearish outlook. But I wouldn't go so far as to say that the company deserves that kind of assessment. Instead, I think that asset quality is enough to offset some of this valuation trouble. In the first chart below, you can see that the institution has a return on assets reading of 1.77%. That's well above the 1% minimum that I typically like to see. It also puts the company above four of the five competitors I stacked it up against. In the chart below that, you can see the same type of comparative analysis using return on equity. Once again, Axos Financial was near the top of the list. And the 15.94% reading that the company boasted exceeded the 10% minimum I typically look for.

Credit quality also is worthy of consideration. This is an area where the institution is a bit worse off than I would like it to be. For instance, 0.74% of its loans are classified as non-accrual. Generally, I like this to be 0.50% or lower. But the good news is that this metric did decline ever so slightly from the 0.79% that was experienced at the end of 2024. So at least we are gradually heading in the right direction.

At the start of this article, I said that management will soon be announcing financial results for the second quarter of the 2026 fiscal year. That is expected to occur after the market closes on January 29th. Revenue for financial institutions is defined as net interest income before any provisions for credit losses, plus non-interest income. During the second quarter of the 2025 fiscal year, Axos Financial reported revenue of $307.9 million. But for this year, it's expected to be higher at $345.9 million. Earnings per share, meanwhile, are forecasted to pop from $1.80 to $2.07. That would push net income up from $104.7 million to $119.6 million. That trend is certainly positive. And if this represents a new normal for the business, it would translate to a price-to-earnings multiple of about 10.9. That's much closer to my comfort level.

Takeaway

As things stand, I think that it's prudent to downgrade Axos Financial from a ‘buy’ to a ‘hold.’ This is because shares are not as cheap as they once were. Credit quality isn't bad, but it's not great either. And even though asset quality is astounding, that alone is not enough in light of the other metrics, particularly the decline in net interest income, to justify anything other than a ‘hold’ rating.

data.title: Rates Spark: Volatility Has Calmed, But There's Lots Going On
data.description:
By Michiel Tukker, Senior European Rates Strategist and Padhraic Garvey, CFA, Regional Head of Research, Americas

US Treasuries are dealing with so much that the path evolving is an unchanged one

Quite a few moving parts to take into consideration.

First, the US dollar is under some pressure. On the other side of that trade are two key elements, the dramatic rally in gold and the snap stronger in the Japanese yen. The latter is manufactured through an intervention threat. But it is also a reflection of prior extreme weakening in the Japanese yen, driven by a policy mix of ultra-low rates for too long, mixed with the inconvenient truth of a mammoth debt/GDP ratio, whose prognosis is too uncomfortable to contemplate. The rally in the price of gold squares the circle, which is, in the end, simply a supply-versus-demand determination. Simple, and offering an alternative to messy alternatives, for e.g. central bank FX reserves.

The implications for treasuries are complicated. At the margin, it likely means more rate cuts and more inflation, and hence a steeper curve from both ends (as a stand-alone impulse).

The Fed meets this week with a green light to not do much at all. While at the same time, DC is shaping up for another government shutdown in the wake of objections from the left following recent events in Minnesota (related to heavy-handed treatment of ICE protestors). That could mean another dearth of government data down the line, making for difficult interpretation of the economy. At the margin, this would place some downward pressure on yields, but not in any dramatic sense.

At the same time, yields have calmed and are not showing huge direction. Lots going on, but we're holding at 4.2% on the 10yr, and feeling quite stable here. But at the same time, vulnerable to getting pushed around. There is clearly demand on the front end though, with the 2yr auction results showing stellar results. The fact that the Fed is buying bonds out to 3yrs is not dispositive, but it is a help.

Low rates volatility makes a supportive environment for carry trades

With rates volatility coming down again, the supportive environment for carry trades seems to be holding. Not a day goes by without the European Central Bank (ECB) reiterating that it is in a “good place”. And indeed, if we look at the volatility of the 2Y swap rate, we can agree that the ECB managed to pull off a soft landing. The risk still seems tilted towards more cuts, but even after last week’s market turmoil, the probability of a cut before June never exceeded 20%. Unless we have a series of lower growth or inflation numbers, we find it hard to see a scenario where markets start moving the front end materially.

The carry positive environment is also reflected in the tight spreads on European Government Bonds (EGBs). The spread between 10Y Italian and German government bonds is hitting new lows and French budget wins quickly translate to tighter spreads too. Our structural view still sees EGB spreads settle wider, but in the near term we also find it difficult to identify catalysts that would trigger a repricing.

Having said that, a period of low rates volatility and the consequent build-up of carry trades can also amplify market shocks in the future. For example, in August 2024, we witnessed a significant risk-off event when the Bank of Japan unexpectedly tightened monetary policy, triggering a global shock to equity markets. Whilst not our baseline, any hawkish surprises from the Fed or ECB monetary policy will be felt by markets.

Rates volatility was only lower when the ECB was at the zero lower bound

Tuesday’s events and market views

Not a lot of notable data from Europe. From the US, we have the Conference Board consumer confidence, which consensus sees ticking up from 89.1 to 90.9. Looking at recent history, though, the trend seems to be down. Other US data includes the Richmond manufacturing index and ADP weekly employment figures.

In terms of supply, we have Germany start with a 20Y syndication for an estimated €5bn. From Italy, we have a 2Y BTP auction, combined with 6Y and 30Y BTPei auctions, together totalling €5bn. Later, Germany will also auction 2Y Schatz for €6bn. Lastly, from the US, we have a 5Y Note auction for $70bn.

data.title: Allstate: Structural Decoupling Of Underwriting Margins From Cyclicality
data.description:
I rate Allstate (ALL) a buy based on a Quality-Value edge. My thesis is focusing on the decoupling of underwriting margins from industry cycles through the ALLIE AI ecosystem. This ecosystem has compressed the actuarial latency in pricing risk. The auto Underlying Combined Ratio of 86 is the new baseline for Allstate’s tech-enabled book. Allstate’s aggressive growth in Active Brands (+2.8% PIF) supports the low-cost distribution model. For my thesis major risks are Float Dilution (high-churn non-standard clients degrade the stability of investable capital) and regulatory hardening in California/New York, capping rate adequacy on the legacy book.

Structural Decoupling of Underwriting Margins from Cyclicality

Wall Street is currently mispricing Allstate stock as a beneficiary of a cyclical hard market, in my opinion. However, Allstate stock should be valued based on the structural shift in its operating leverage. Allstate stock is trading at a P/E of ~7.4x despite a TTM ROE of 34.7% and a Q3-FY2025 Combined Ratio [CR] of 80.1. The market may be assuming mean reversion to historical profitability levels. I think this view is flawed because it ignores the Algorithmic Velocity that emerged from the Transformative Growth strategy and the ALLIE ecosystem. To back this up, historically, insurance profitability suffered from actuarial latency (the time lag between recognizing loss cost inflation and implementing rate earn-in). Allstate has compressed this latency to near zero, and it is creating a stable moat that justifies a valuation re-rating closer to peers like Progressive (PGR).

Moreso, the bullish catalyst is the link between the Affordable, Simple, Connected [ASC] product architecture (now in 42 states) and Agentic AI. In Q3, there is an observable decoupling happening. Despite social inflation and severity, Allstate ticked an underlying auto CR of 86 and homeowners CR of 59.8 (underlying). This is happening beyond rate hikes (only +0.6% annualized impact in Q3), due to the precision of risk stratification. By utilizing digital roof imagery on every home and telematics-based pricing in auto, Allstate is shedding unpriced risk while holding priced risk. The expense ratio reduction of 6.7 points since 2018 is the conversion of fixed legacy labor into variable, scalable technology costs.

To clarify more, the state of Active Brands vs. Inactive Brands signals a potent growth factor. Although total auto PIF grew only 1.3% Y/Y and Active Brands (National General + Direct) grew 2.8% Y/Y, Allstate’s new business surged 26.2% Y/Y. This trend indicates that the high-margin tech-enabled book is displacing the lower-margin legacy book. As the Inactive drag (Esurance/Encompass down ~30-38%) removes from the denominator over the next 4-6 quarters, the headline growth rate will merge with the 26.2% new business pace. This creates a double-compounder effect. The effect is that accelerating revenue growth on a permanently lower fixed-cost base can drive book value expansion beyond the current $95.95/share. Wall Street is pricing in a peak, but the above factors indicate a new higher step of capital generation.

The Quality of Float Dilution Risk

The main risk, in my opinion for Allstate stock is way ahead of the obvious threat of catastrophes. This risk is the growing fragility in the quality of its insurance float based on the aggressive shift in distribution channels. This Float Dilution creates a divergence (long-term) between GAAP earnings and actual economic value added [EVA]. In Q3, I see a risky trend. Although Allstate pursues Transformative Growth, it is swapping high-retention high-LTV (Lifetime Value) captive agency clients for low-retention price-sensitive independent agent [IA] and direct clients.

To support this, I want to point out the disparity between new business velocity (+26.2%) and net Policies in Force [PIF] growth (+1.3%). This big churn gap indicates that Allstate is spending a lot ($2.2 billion in marketing LTM, up from $0.9 billion in 2019) to acquire clients and these clients have shorter retention lifecycles. The growth in National General (12%) and Direct Auto (22.9%) confirms the portfolio is shifting toward non-standard and price-shopping demographics. Against the legacy Allstate Agency book [provided stable multi-decade capital (float) that allowed for long-duration investing], this new client cohort is volatile. This forces the investment portfolio ($82.3 billion) to maintain higher liquidity and shorter duration to match the liability profile. This is structurally compressing net investment income yields over time relative to comps with stickier books (logically).

Along with that, the regulatory friction in major states (NY/NJ/CA) builds a profitability trap. Although Allstate has returned to profitability in these regions, the inability to roll out the ASC product there (transcript) means these large markets remain on legacy systems with legacy pricing models. This segregates Allstate into a high-tech agile entity in 42 states and a legacy regulated utility in 3 major markets. Now the risk is if inflation re-accelerates, the short-duration of the new client base can lead to rapid defecting (shopping). At the same time, the regulatory ceiling in NY/NJ/CA will prevent rate adequacy on the sticky legacy book. In my opinion, this squeeze can collapse the spread between ROE (34.7%) and Cost of Equity (8%, computed below). It means that current buybacks ($360 million in Q3) are executed at peak cycle valuation on lower-quality forward earnings lines.

Precise Company-Specific Long-Term Stock Valuation Analysis

To value Allstate stock, I focus on the structural break in operating efficiency. Standard Dividend Discount Model [DDM] fails here as it does not consider the Algorithmic Velocity of the Transformative Growth strategy. The strategy has permanently lowered the expense ratio equilibrium by 670 basis points since 2018. For a more accurate valuation, I use a Modified Excess Return Model, adjusting for the Active Brand displacement. With a current Book Value of $95.95 and an adjusted ROE of 34.7%, Allstate is generating excess returns of ~26.7% over a 8% Cost of Equity.

Even at a normalization of ROE to 20% (assuming ~6.7% lower based on the lower retention of the new Direct book and inevitable catastrophe reversion, as it is technically a sound average). So the Excess Return Spread is [Normalized ROE (20%) - Cost of Equity (8%)] = 12%. This gives the Excess EPS [12% Spread × $95.95 BVPS] of $11.5. With all these, the value of Excess Return [I apply a perpetuity growth formula using a conservative 1% long-term growth rate (g). It is half of the forecasted US GDP growth to discount the high-churn nature of the new book.

Therefore, the Terminal Value of Excess Returns [Excess EPS / (CoE - g)] is $164.3. This leads to an Intrinsic Value [Current Book Value ($95.95) + Value of Excess Returns ($164.3)] of $260.25. What bulls should not ignore is that the investment portfolio ($82.3 billion) is now yielding higher income ($949 million in Q3, +21.2% YoY) without increased duration risk. In my opinion, this income builds a double-compounder effect on Book Value that can also support the derived ALL stock value of $260.25 (+34.4% price upside).

Allstate Stock Pre-Q4-2025 and Post-Q4-2025 Earnings Alpha Protocol

Bulls can accumulate long-positions pre-Q4 earnings. I see a big beat as October and November catastrophe losses totaled only $129 million. Estimates for December ($80 million) indicate a quarterly catastrophe load of ~$209 million. It is largely below the $558 million in Q3. This means Q4 EPS can exceed $14 (over Q3’s EPS) and the consensus high of $11.36. After-earnings, ALL stock volatility may occur if management guides conservatively on auto severity inflation. Bull can use any post-release dip to average down the long-positions as the subsequent capital return announcement (share buyback reset) in early 2026 may serve as the next major catalyst for multiple expansion.

__

All in all, I rate ALL stock as a Buy based on the disparity between a 6.4x P/E ratio and a structural ROE that is not sustainable. The market efficiency can re-rate Allstate stock valuation upward, in my opinion, to be in line with sector multiples as the Inactive Brand drag removes. Moving forward, I will closely track the spread between New Issued Applications (+26.2%) and Net PIF Growth (+1.3%). If this churn gap widens further, it indicates the cost of growth is eroding the quality of float. This will be a red flag for the thesis. I will also keep an eye on monthly catastrophe reports for deviation from the sub-$200 million trend.

data.title: Pfizer: The Great Healthcare Plan Does Not Change My Bullish Stance
data.description:
I only own a handful of healthcare stocks. Given my preference to invest in small-cap stocks, most of these stocks are very thinly covered even on Seeking Alpha, which, in my opinion, is the best place on the Internet for small-cap research. Pfizer, Inc. (PFE) is one of the two big pharma stocks I own. The other one is Vertex Pharmaceuticals, Inc. (VRTX).

PFE stock has gained some momentum in the recent past, but a major breakthrough to the upside has not occurred yet. When I first invested in PFE, I thought the market was underestimating the company's potential to cut back on large investments to drive earnings higher. This was more than 2 years ago. I have added to my position at regular intervals since then, but I am hardly at the breakeven point with my PFE investment still.

On January 15, President Trump introduced a new healthcare framework to lower healthcare costs in the U.S., named the Great Healthcare Plan. This reminds us that the threat of adverse policy developments is far from over. However, as I discuss in this analysis, I believe Pfizer has what it takes to come out unscathed from these policy pressures and thrive in the long run.

A Closer Look At The Great Healthcare Plan

If you are a pharma investor, keeping a close eye on new regulatory developments is key, especially today, given the rapid pace at which new reforms are introduced. In the first year in Office, President Trump has already signed 2 impactful reforms into law. I am not planning to discuss these reforms in detail here, but I thought it best to summarize the potential impact of these 2 reforms on Pfizer's financial performance.

Policy reform Comments Impact on Pfizer
Medicare Part D redesign under the Inflation Reduction Act Drug manufacturers are now liable to offer a 20% discount on brand-name drugs in the catastrophic phase of coverage. Pfizer management has guided for a $1 billion impact from this reform in 2025 alone.
The One Big Beautiful Act The corporate effective tax rate was lowered by this bill. This is a tailwind for Pfizer. In Q3, the company reported a negative effective tax rate.

Source: Company filings

In addition to these major reforms, I believe PFE investors need to keep a close eye on ongoing Section 232 investigations as well.

Coming back to the Great Healthcare Plan, this framework was introduced to achieve 4 main objectives. You can read the full text here.

  1. Lower drug prices.
  2. Lower insurance premiums.
  3. Hold insurance companies accountable on all fronts.
  4. Maximize drug price transparency.

In the next segment, I will discuss why Pfizer is in a strong position to weather the negative impact from these policy changes and thrive in the long run.

The Great Healthcare Plan Will Not Push Pfizer Into A Corner

For Pfizer, the most direct negative impact will arise from the Trump administration's efforts to lower drug prices. As part of the Great Healthcare Plan, the goal is to ensure Americans get the same prescription drug prices offered to people in other countries. In a nutshell, Pfizer (and other drug manufacturers) will have a ceiling on drug prices. The company's pricing power will no longer be as strong as it has been in the past. While this is not an encouraging development, pharma investors have to deal with this reality going forward.

The Great Healthcare Plan's objective to hold insurance companies accountable on all fronts could actually turn out to be a net positive for Pfizer. It is standard practice for a drug maker to increase the list prices of drugs to cover the substantial kickbacks (in the form of rebates) to pharmacy benefit managers. The Great Healthcare Plan aims to end kickbacks from PBMs. If this is achieved, Pfizer could potentially lower drug prices to meet the government's demands without actually having to dent its revenue materially. The Great Healthcare Plan's stated goal is to hold insurance companies accountable and therefore could actually turn out to be a hedge for the threat posed by lower drug prices. I will monitor the pharma space closely to see how the Trump administration moves forward to bring the Great Healthcare Plan alive, to evaluate if Pfizer could benefit from restrictions on insurance companies.

The Great Healthcare Plan also includes an interesting statement: voluntarily negotiated deals with HHS/CMS will be grandfathered in.

Last September, Pfizer reached an agreement with the Trump administration to lower drug prices voluntarily, making it the first pharma company to voluntarily negotiate a deal with the government. This agreement covers almost all the requirements highlighted by President Trump in a letter in July 2025, calling for pharma companies to offer favorable drug pricing to Americans.

  1. Pfizer agreed to offer most-favored-nation pricing for its drugs in Medicaid.
  2. The company also agreed to offer discounts on direct sales of its drugs.
  3. Self-enforcing a ceiling on new drug prices, Pfizer also agreed to ensure pricing parity with developed markets for all of its new drug launches.

Although a hit on Pfizer's pricing power is unavoidable, the good news is that Pfizer has more clarity on both pricing and tariffs thanks to this voluntary agreement. In return for Pfizer agreeing to lower drug prices, the Trump administration has agreed to provide Pfizer a 3-year grace period to exempt the company from Section 232 tariffs. The catch is that Pfizer would have to invest in the U.S. to bring manufacturing onshore, which the company is already doing. In the absence of this deal, Pfizer was facing a potential 100% tariff on active pharmaceutical ingredients manufactured in its global locations.

This agreement also allows Pfizer to sell directly on TrumpRx.gov, which is expected to be launched soon. For those who are not familiar with TrumpRx.gov, this is a new platform where drug manufacturers will be able to directly sell prescription drugs to patients, bypassing PBMs (albeit at a discounted rate).

In addition to this, Pfizer's voluntary agreement makes the company immune to stricter restrictions that may be introduced under the Great Healthcare Plan in the future. This is because pharma companies that have voluntarily agreed to slash drug prices will be grandfathered in.

Bull Thesis Remains Intact

My bullish stance on Pfizer stems from the strength of the company's pipeline, the ongoing integration of its recent acquisitions (Metsera and Seagen), and its cheap valuation. I believe Mr. Market is still oblivious to how the company is setting itself up for stellar long-term earnings growth by replacing its patent-expiring blockbusters with newcomers. You can read my previous analyses for an in-depth view of my bullish stance.

In a nutshell, I believe Pfizer's current valuation is detached from the economic reality facing the company. At a forward P/E of just 8.2x (a significant discount to the sector median of ~18x), Pfizer is valued as if it is already past peak earnings, when in reality, 2026 is shaping up to be a very important year for the company from a new drug development perspective.

Key catalysts expected in 2026

You can check the full development pipeline here.

As I have discussed in my previous analyses, I believe Pfizer's development pipeline is home to a few drugs with blockbuster potential, which alleviates the threat posed by some near-term patent expirations. The good thing is that Pfizer is rewarding investors handsomely to patiently wait until this drug portfolio transformation is complete. The current dividend, which yields 6.7%, is well supported by the $4.5 billion the company saved with its cost realignment program in addition to its operating cash flows.

Although my bullish stance is unshattered by the Great Healthcare Plan, I believe investors should keep their eyes on several risks, including the threat of adverse policy developments. I am monitoring how Pfizer will be impacted by discounted drug pricing in 2026 to evaluate the impact on operating margins. I am also keeping an eye on Pfizer's U.S. investments to determine how these investments will impact ROE. In addition, I believe investors need to monitor the increased tax risk arising from onshoring manufacturing.

Q4 Earnings Preview

Pfizer is scheduled to report Q4 2025 earnings on February 3. Consensus estimates point to EPS of 57 cents for Q4 on revenue of $16.83 billion. More than the headline numbers, I believe investors should pay close attention to Pfizer's guidance for 2026. Any change to the current 2026 guidance is likely to be the biggest driver of PFE stock in the post-earnings trading session. As a long-term-oriented shareholder, I am looking forward to the upcoming earnings call where the management is likely to give color on a few important developments.

  1. Commentary on the progress of the Metsera integration. Pfizer's obesity drug plans largely revolve around Metsera.
  2. Any commentary on the expected margin impact from the voluntary agreement it reached with the government.
  3. Commentary on the plans for a resumption in buybacks.
  4. Commentary on CapEx growth to meet the requirements in its agreement with the government to bring manufacturing onshore.

Takeaway

Pfizer is one of the few big pharma companies I own. Although the Great Healthcare Plan renews fears of adverse policy developments, I believe 2026 will be a year when Mr. Market shifts its focus from regulatory developments to Pfizer's pipeline once again. When this happens, I expect long-term shareholders to be rewarded handsomely.
